metadata.purpose: Standardized categorization of CISSP exam traps and trick questions

NEG.name: Negative Modifiers
NEG.the_trap: You skim over words like LEAST, EXCEPT, NOT, or BEST and answer the obvious positive truth.
NEG.the_fix: Flip the stem. Re-read the question replacing LEAST with MOST to see if your answer still makes sense.
NEG.isc2_principle: Careful reading of negative modifiers in question stems
NEG.example_trap: Which is the LEAST effective control against availability threats? → Pick Encryption
NEG.example_correct: Which is the LEAST effective control against availability threats? → Pick Backups
NEG.prevention_strategy: Circle all negative modifiers before reading options

ABS.name: Absolute Language
ABS.the_trap: You pick answers with Always, Never, All, or Completely Eliminate because they sound decisive and confident.
ABS.the_fix: Eliminate immediately. In risk/security, 100% certainty is impossible. The correct answer will almost always use Generally, Risk-based, or Mitigate.
ABS.isc2_principle: Risk management is probabilistic, not deterministic
ABS.example_trap: This will completely prevent SQL injection → Sounds authoritative
ABS.example_correct: This will significantly reduce the risk of SQL injection → Realistic
ABS.prevention_strategy: Eliminate answers with Always, Never, All, Completely, Impossible

ROLE.name: Job Title Mismatch
ROLE.the_trap: You pick a technical fix (firewall/antivirus) even when the stem gives you a non-technical job title.
ROLE.the_fix: Match the role. Analyst = Technical. Manager = Policy/Process. Owner = Approve budget/risk. Custodian = Implement the fix.
ROLE.example_trap: Data Owner should handle? → Install a DLP agent (IT Admin job)
ROLE.example_correct: Data Owner should? → Approve the new Data Classification Policy
ROLE.prevention_strategy: Map each role to its authority level before reading options

ORDER.name: Process Sequence
ORDER.the_trap: You jump to the fix or solution without considering the mandatory step that comes before it.
ORDER.the_fix: Recite the ISC2 lifecycle. BCP = BIA first. Incident Response = Containment first. Forensics = Capture RAM first.
ORDER.isc2_principle: Understanding SDLC, BCP, incident response, and forensic lifecycles
ORDER.example_trap: First step in BCP activation? → Restore from the last backup
ORDER.example_correct: First step in BCP activation? → Activate the warm site per the DR Plan (comes after BIA)
ORDER.prevention_strategy: Write out the lifecycle sequence before reading options

SCOPE.name: Boundary Confusion
SCOPE.the_trap: You apply on-premise logic to cloud, or forget who holds the legal liability in shared environments.
SCOPE.the_fix: Define the shared model. In IaaS, you manage OS/Apps. In PaaS, you manage Data/Apps. In SaaS, you manage only Data & Access.
SCOPE.example_trap: In IaaS, reconfigure the hypervisor settings
SCOPE.example_correct: In IaaS, reconfigure the guest OS firewall (your responsibility, not provider's)
SCOPE.prevention_strategy: Identify the deployment model first, then assign responsibility

ALL.name: Umbrella Effect
ALL.the_trap: You pick a specific, technically correct answer (e.g., Firewall) when a broader answer covers it entirely.
ALL.the_fix: Ask: Does this include the others? If yes, pick the broadest strategic answer, not the tactical one.
ALL.example_trap: Best overall security strategy? → Intrusion Detection System
ALL.example_correct: Best overall security strategy? → Defense-in-Depth strategy (includes IDS)
ALL.prevention_strategy: Read all options before choosing, look for broader answers

GOLD.name: Shiny Object
GOLD.the_trap: You pick the most complex, technical, or crypto-related term because it looks impressive, even if it's irrelevant to the question.
GOLD.the_fix: Eliminate irrelevant domains. If the question is about Authentication, ignore anything related to Encryption (CIA triad mismatch).
GOLD.example_trap: Identity proofing approach? → Use AES-256 encryption (impressive but wrong domain)
GOLD.example_correct: Identity proofing? → Implement a one-time password (OTP) token (authentication-specific)
GOLD.prevention_strategy: Identify the domain of the question before reading options

ETHIC.name: Moral Hazard
ETHIC.the_trap: You pick the cool vigilante option (hacking back) or the quick legal option (calling cops) without considering professional duty.
ETHIC.the_fix: Apply ISC2 Canon. Protect society first. Then protect the organization. Then inform authorities. Never take offensive action.
ETHIC.example_trap: Trace the attacker's IP and block them globally (hacking back)
ETHIC.example_correct: Isolate the compromised system and preserve logs for chain of custody
ETHIC.prevention_strategy: Remember ISC2 Canon: Society > Organization > Authorities > Actions

EASY.name: Overthink
EASY.the_trap: The CAT gives you a simple, obvious question early on. You assume it's a trick and overcomplicate it.
EASY.the_fix: Trust the obvious. If a question seems easy, it's because you are performing well. Pick the straightforward, textbook answer.
EASY.example_trap: What is authentication? → Assume trick, pick Retinal Scan
EASY.example_correct: What is authentication? → Multi-Factor Authentication (textbook answer)
EASY.prevention_strategy: Trust obvious answers when performing well in CAT

TIME.name: Clock Killer
TIME.the_trap: You spend 4 minutes on a single, highly complex question because you're afraid to get it wrong.
TIME.the_fix: Eliminate 2 wrongs, guess, and move on. Getting one hard question wrong won't tank you; running out of time will.
TIME.example_trap: Re-reading a complex scenario 5 times, changing answer multiple times
TIME.example_correct: Read once, eliminate 2 wrongs, pick between 2 remaining, click Next
TIME.prevention_strategy: Set 90-second timer per question, move on if stuck

REPEAT.name: Deja Vu
REPEAT.the_trap: The CAT gives you a question on a topic you already saw. You assume you got it wrong previously.
REPEAT.the_fix: Treat it as a new question. CAT recycles domains. Do not assume your previous answer was wrong. Read it fresh.
REPEAT.isc2_principle: Understanding CAT algorithm and domain recycling
REPEAT.example_trap: Got BCP earlier, must have failed, so pick different answer now
REPEAT.example_correct: Apply ORDER rule fresh to this new incident response question
REPEAT.prevention_strategy: Treat each question independently, ignore prior similar questions

COMPLIANCE.name: Regulatory Confusion
COMPLIANCE.the_trap: You mix up which regulation applies to which scenario. (GDPR vs CCPA vs SOX vs HIPAA vs PCI-DSS vs ISO 27001)
COMPLIANCE.the_fix: GDPR = EU personal data. CCPA = California. HIPAA = US healthcare. PCI-DSS = Payment cards. SOX = Public companies. ISO 27001 = General framework.
COMPLIANCE.example_trap: EU customer data + US company = Implement CCPA controls
COMPLIANCE.example_correct: EU customer data = Implement GDPR controls (regardless of company location)
COMPLIANCE.prevention_strategy: Create a regulatory framework comparison table

HIERARCHY.name: Authority Mismatch
HIERARCHY.the_trap: You suggest the wrong person has authority to make a decision. (CEO ≠ CISO ≠ Data Owner ≠ Security Officer)
HIERARCHY.the_fix: CEO = Budget. CISO = Strategy. Data Owner = Data decisions. Security Officer = Implementation. System Owner = System decisions.
HIERARCHY.example_trap: CISO approves all access requests directly
HIERARCHY.example_correct: Data Owner approves access to their data; CISO defines the process

TOOL.name: Wrong Technology
TOOL.the_trap: You pick a tool that's technically sound but isn't the BEST fit for the specific scenario.
TOOL.the_fix: Context matters: Firewalls stop network attacks, not insider threats. DLP catches data exfiltration. SIEM detects patterns.
TOOL.isc2_principle: Matching security tools to specific threats
TOOL.example_trap: Stop insider data theft = Install firewall
TOOL.example_correct: Stop insider data theft = Implement DLP (Data Loss Prevention)

CONTEXT.name: Missing Scenario Clues
CONTEXT.the_trap: You miss a critical detail in the scenario that changes the answer. (Internal/external, regulated, startup, cloud, etc.)
CONTEXT.the_fix: Highlight scenario context: Internal vs External, Regulated vs Non-regulated, Startup vs Mature, Cloud vs On-premise.
CONTEXT.example_trap: Scenario says THIRD-PARTY cloud → Answer assumes you own infrastructure
CONTEXT.example_correct: Third-party cloud = Answer must reference shared responsibility model
CONTEXT.prevention_strategy: Highlight context clues in scenario before reading options

DEFINITION.name: Concept Confusion
DEFINITION.the_trap: You confuse two similar concepts. (Authentication ≠ Authorization; Threat ≠ Risk; Vulnerability ≠ Exploit)
DEFINITION.the_fix: Create flashcards: Authentication = Prove identity. Authorization = Grant permissions. Threat = Something that could happen. Risk = Probability × Impact.

LIFECYCLE.name: Process Stage Error
LIFECYCLE.the_trap: You answer with the correct technique, but at the wrong stage of a process lifecycle.
LIFECYCLE.the_fix: BCP: BIA → Plan → Implement → Test → Maintain. Incident: Detect → Contain → Eradicate → Recover → Post-mortem. SDLC: Design → Develop → Test → Deploy → Maintain.
LIFECYCLE.example_trap: First step in incident response? → Eradicate the threat
LIFECYCLE.example_correct: First step in incident response? → Contain (stop spreading)
LIFECYCLE.prevention_strategy: Memorize and visualize each lifecycle sequence

METRIC.name: Measurement Mismatch
METRIC.the_trap: You pick a metric that's important but isn't what the question is asking for.
METRIC.the_fix: RTO = Recovery time. RPO = Recovery point (data loss window). MTBF = Mean time between failures. MTTR = Mean time to repair. SLA = Uptime %.
METRIC.example_correct: Measure DR readiness = Verify RTO/RPO targets met
METRIC.prevention_strategy: Create metrics reference card (RTO, RPO, MTBF, MTTR, SLA)

ASSUMPTION.name: Unstated Prerequisites
ASSUMPTION.the_trap: You assume something the question doesn't state, and your answer breaks in edge cases.
ASSUMPTION.the_fix: Check: Is this answer valid ONLY if [assumption is true]? Look for answers that work in MOST cases.
ASSUMPTION.example_trap: Secure transmission = Encrypt with TLS (assumes infrastructure exists)
ASSUMPTION.example_correct: Secure transmission = Encrypt with TLS AND verify certificate
ASSUMPTION.prevention_strategy: Add second layer to single-layer answers

VERSUS.name: Similar Options
VERSUS.the_trap: Two answers are both technically correct, but one is MORE correct (best practice vs practical vs theoretical).
VERSUS.the_fix: Context: True vs Best vs Most Practical. Ask what the question is really asking for.
VERSUS.isc2_principle: Understanding ISC2 standards and best practices
VERSUS.prevention_strategy: Compare all options against current ISC2 standards

TIMING.name: When vs What
TIMING.the_trap: You understand WHAT happens, but miss WHEN it happens in a sequence.
TIMING.the_fix: Create timeline diagrams: Incident Response → Forensics → BCP activation. Pay attention to 'first,' 'before,' 'after' keywords.
TIMING.example_trap: During forensics, when do you analyze memory? → During analyze phase
TIMING.example_correct: First action during forensics? → Capture volatile memory (RAM) before disk
TIMING.prevention_strategy: Create timeline diagrams for each major process
